text: 3 © 2024 GMT Games, LLC Space Empires: All Good Things - Competitive Scenario Book - Version 1.0 1.0 Foreword This book contains all the competitive scenarios for Space Empires 4X, Close Encounters, Replicators, and All Good Things. The base game and both previous expansions are necessary to take advantage of all the options found here. These rules assume that you are using the updated player aid cards provided with All Good Things. Cross-references to sections will be included in parentheses, e.g., (5.7). References to the Master Rule Book and the Solo/Co-Op Scenario Book will be notated accordingly, e.g. (MRB 5.5) or (SSB 8.0). 1.1 STANDARD RULES FOR COMPETITIVE SCENARIOS 1.1.1 Player At-Start Forces: Unless otherwise specified, in each scenario each player gets the following: • A fully developed 30 CP Homeworld. When playing with the optional rules for Facilities (MRB 36.0), the 20 CP Homeworld counter is used instead. • Three Colony Ships, one Miner, four Shipyards (SY), and three Scouts (SC) at their Homeworld. • One Flagship (optional, MRB 23.0). 1.1.2 Customizable: Give each rule a shot before you judge it. A lot of thought and play went into them. However, once familiar with the game, feel free to play the game any way you want – this game is meant to be customized. You won’t offend the designer! 1.1.3 Galaxy Setup: Unless otherwise specified, Home System markers of a player’s color are placed in hexes that represent their home territory and Deep Space System markers (white) are placed in all other hexes – even hexes that could possibly have been a Home System area if there were more players. Excess System markers are set aside. 1.1.4 Warp Points and Doomsday Machines: Warp Point and Doomsday Machine counters should not be removed from the game at setup. However, unless you are playing with the appropriate optional rule (MRB 28.2; MRB 29.0), they are considered “open space” and are removed from the game when flipped. Feel free to do this with every counter players agree not to use in their game. 1.1.5 2-Player Victory Condition: The victory condition for each scenario, unless stated otherwise, is to destroy your opponent’s Homeworld. However, the winning side can often become obvious before that, and it is suggested that you end the game at that point. 1.1.6 Multiplayer Victory Condition: Victory in multiplayer games, unless stated otherwise, is to destroy one opponent’s Homeworld. When any player destroys any other player’s Homeworld, they become the victor. 1.1.7 Multiplayer Short Game Victory Condition (Colony Points): In addition to the condition in 1.1.6 above: • A player wins by scoring 3 Colony Points. A player gets a Colony Point by: - Destroying (not damaging) another player’s Colony. This includes new Colonies, but not Colony Ships. Once gained, this Colony Point cannot be lost. Capturing another player’s Colony using ground combat also counts towards this requirement (MRB 21.0). - By having a 5 CP Colony in Deep Space or an opponent’s Home System. Unlike points earned by

subject: Board Games / 4X Strategy / Space Empires 4X / Scenarios & Variants
text: Colony. This includes new Colonies, but not Colony Ships. Once gained, this Colony Point cannot be lost. Capturing another player’s Colony using ground combat also counts towards this requirement (MRB 21.0). - By having a 5 CP Colony in Deep Space or an opponent’s Home System. Unlike points earned by destroying Colonies, this Colony Point is not permanent. A player has it only for so long as they have a 5 CP Colony in those sections of the map. - A captured Colony may count for up to two Colony Points. Capturing another player’s Colony yields 1 point. If that Colony grows to 5 CP, and it is outside of the player’s Home System, it would then count as an additional point (like any other Colony outside the player’s Home System). The point for capturing it can never be lost, but the point for having a Colony at 5 CP could be. If a player recaptures a Colony that previously belonged to them it does not yield a Colony Point for destroying a colony, but may yield a colony point for growing to 5 CP if outside the Home System. • A player wins the instant they score their third Colony Point and the game ends immediately, even if it is mid-turn. • If multiple players score their third Colony Point in the same Economic Phase, the win goes to the player with the most Colony Points. In the event of a tie, after the purchases for the Economic Phase are completed, the players add up the CP value of all combat-capable ships they have on the board and the winner is the player whose fleet adds up to the most CP. In the event of another tie, the tied players share the win. • If playing with Variable Map setup (3.2, 4.2), Colony Points are only scored for destroying Colonies – not for having them. 1.1.8 Multiplayer Long Game Victory Condition: Players are eliminated when their Homeworld is destroyed. Remove all of their pieces and Colonies. Yes, we realize that their civilization would not disappear when this happens. This represents them ceasing to function as a political entity. The mechanism to bring the individual planets into your empire is represented by colonization. The winner is the last player standing. A player that eliminates another player gets a bonus 30 CPs in the next Economic Phase. If the player they conquered has technology that they do not possess, they may choose one level of two separate technologies from that player and mark them as researched on their own sheet. Homeworlds that are re-colonized by other players act as normal Colonies (they normally produce at most 5 CPs).

subject: Board Games / 4X Strategy / Space Empires 4X / Scenarios & Variants
text: 4 © 2024 GMT Games, LLC Space Empires: All Good Things - Competitive Scenario Book - Version 1.0 1.1.9 “Sinister” Victory Condition: A player wins by eliminating the Homeworld of the player on their left. This creates two fronts for each player (one offensive and one defensive) and reduces the likelihood of two players ganging up to eliminate a third. The archaic definition of sinister is “related to the left side/left-handed.” The notion that left- handedness was a sign of sin/evil has led to the modern usage of sinister to mean so. 1.1.10 Simultaneous Play: To speed up play (so that you can play more games!), many parts of Space Empires were designed to be done simultaneously. Much of the early turns can be done simultaneously. When playing with experienced players, it is suggested (if there is no threat of direct interaction) that all players conduct their turns at once and only wait for people in order to keep everyone in the same Economic Phase. It is recommended to switch to sequential turns at the latest when only two rows of deep space remain unexplored between players. 1.1.11 Quick Start Variant: Use this suggestion for quicker play. After setting up, flip all Home System markers and place a full-strength Colony on all non-barren planets. In this scenario, the players do not start with any Colony Ships. 1.1.12 (Small) Handicap Rule: Use this rule to provide a small advantage to a less-experienced player by increasing their CP income during the early game. During each Economic Phase, the income of that player’s Colonies will increase by two steps. For example, a Colony Ship is flipped to its Colony side and a “3 Colony” marker is placed on top instead of a “1 Colony.” 1.1.13 Variable but Balanced Home Systems: In this variant, all players have the same markers in their Home Systems, but no player is aware of which markers are in play. • All players set aside the following markers from their Home Systems: two non-barren Planets, two 5-Mineral counters, one Asteroid, and one Nebula. • Each player then also sets aside all 8 of the new Home System markers (these are marked with a “v” on the front side): one Supernova, one Fold in Space, two 10-Mineral counters, one Space Wreck, one Space Pirate, and two 10 CP Planets (see below). • All these counters are organized into stacks of one counter of each type per player. This will create 14 stacks. EXAMPLE: In a 3-player game, there will be 14 stacks of 3 counters each – one from each player: One stack with one Asteroids from each player; two stacks with one 10-Mineral counter from each player, etc. • Players collectively choose 6 of the 14 stacks. These stacks should be chosen randomly and blindly so all players are unaware of which markers will be included in the game. Each player takes their 6 markers and shuffles them back into their Home System markers before setting up their Home

subject: Board Games / 4X Strategy / Space Empires 4X / Scenarios & Variants
text: Players collectively choose 6 of the 14 stacks. These stacks should be chosen randomly and blindly so all players are unaware of which markers will be included in the game. Each player takes their 6 markers and shuffles them back into their Home System markers before setting up their Home System. 10 CP Planets will grow to 5 CP like any other Colony and will then grow to 10 CP the following phase. At 5 CP, place a flipped Colony Ship on top; remove it when the Colony reaches 10 CP. 1.2 GAMEPLAY ADVICE The play time for this game is in the 1 to 4-hour range for a 2-player game, depending on the scenario. For multiplayer games, the play time will be at the higher end of that range. Since new players tend to take longer the first couple of times they play any game, here are some suggestions to keep play time down in the early games. • Start with just the basic rules. While they are fairly simple, the game offers a lot of choices and decisions. Starting with just the basic rules simplifies some of the choices and makes the first few Economic Phases easier to wrap your head around. • Start with the Small 2-Player Scenario. This is a scenario that experienced players can finish in less than an hour and a half. Even if it takes a new player an hour longer than that, it is easily manageable. • For the first multiplayer game, play teams. It will drastically shorten the playing time as you are learning the game. Try to pair an experienced player with a beginner if possible. • Be aggressive! This is a game that rewards aggressive play. 1.3 PLAYER ALLIANCES Some 3-Player or 4-Player scenarios include rules called UNEASY ALLIANCE or BLOOD BROTHERS. When using either of these rules in any scenario, teammates win or lose the game together. Also, you may not capture Colonies from your allies with Ground Units (MRB 21.0) nor may you use Boarding Ships (MRB 19.0) on your allies. You may never bombard Colonies that your allies originally colonized. 1.3.1 UNEASY ALLIANCE: Teammates may never stack units of any kind in the same hex and may not use each other’s MS Pipelines. 1.3.2 BLOOD BROTHERS: Teammates execute their turns at the same time, may stack together, may attack together, and may use each other’s MS Pipelines for movement. However, they may not share research or CPs. 1.4 DEFINITIONS FOR ALL SCENARIOS • Homeworld setup: Unless otherwise noted in the scenario rules, Homeworlds may be placed in a player’s Home System hex marked with a red “H” or a red-dashed line. • Hexes outside of the playing area are NOT playable.

2.0 2-Player Scenarios

2.1 2-PLAYER SMALL

• This game is good for introducing players to the system. It is also

good for experienced players when they have a time constraint.

• Half-sized hexes on the right side of the playing area are NOT

playable (see exception below).

• There are 17 Deep Space markers.

• There are 26 Home System markers on each side. This means that

Player 2 must choose one of the half-hexes on the right edge of

their playing area within their Home System. This half-hex gets

one of the System markers and is fully playable during the game.

2.2 2-PLAYER STANDARD

• Half-sized hexes on the right side of the playing area are NOT

playable (see exception below).

• There are 34 Deep Space markers.

• There are 26 Home System markers on each side. This means that

Player 1 must choose one of the half-hexes on the right edge of

their playing area within their Home System. This half-hex gets

one of the System markers and is fully playable during the game.

• It is recommended that you use Non-Player Aliens (MRB 18.0).

2.3 2-PLAYER LARGE

• Half-sized hexes on the right side of the playing area are NOT

playable (see exception below).

• There are 51 Deep Space markers.

• There are 26 Home System markers on each side. This means that

Player 2 must choose one of the half-hexes on the right edge of

their playing area within their Home System. This half-hex gets

one of the System markers and is fully playable during the game.

• Yeah, you might want to research Exploration technology…

2.4 2-PLAYER EXTRA LARGE

• Each player sets up their 26 Home System markers in one of

the regions normally used in a 4-Player game.

• There are 98 Deep Space markers.

2.5 2-PLAYER “3D”

The title of this scenario promises more than it gives, but you’ll

see what we mean.

• Each player sets up their 26 Home System markers in one of

the regions normally used in a 4-Player game.

• There are 46 Deep Space markers.

• The two unused home regions get no System markers – they

are not in play.

• More than not being in play, the unused home regions need

to be thought of as not being there. A player may move a ship

into an unused home region and come out in any other hex

adjacent to that unused home region. In essence, it is as if all

the hexes adjacent to an unused home region are adjacent to

each other.

• This is an attempt to introduce a 3D element to the game

board. Yes, it’s crude. Yes, it’s simple. But we think it’s clever. ☺

2.6 2-PLAYER MASSIVE

• Each player sets up their 26 Home System markers as suggested

by the diagram. There are no guides on the game board to

indicate this set up.

• Player 1 will have one Home System marker and Player 2

will have six Home System markers that do not fit within the

area indicated by the line. These should be placed in any hex

adjacent to that area.

• There are 98 Deep Space markers.

2.7 “I DARE YOU TO PLAY THIS”

• Each player sets up their 26 Home System markers as suggested

by the diagram. There are no guides on the game board to

indicate this set up.

• The first two rows closest to each player will be filled by 25 of

their Home System markers. The remaining marker should be

placed in any hex adjacent to that area.

• There are 98 Deep Space markers.

2.8 2-PLAYER DOUBLE TROUBLE

• Each player uses all the pieces of their color, plus the Home

System markers of another color. One non-Barren planet and

• The Home System markers of both colors are mixed together,

and each player sets up these 50 Home System markers as

board to indicate this set up.

• The first four rows closest to each player will be filled by their

50 Home System markers.

• There are 50 Deep Space markers.

• Each player can use the Colony counters of either of their colors.

2.9 2-PLAYER KNIFE FIGHT

• Setup: All 26 Home System markers are used for both players.

However, no Deep Space markers are used. One player sets

up their Home System markers in one of the four quadrants

used in the 4-Player game (4.0). The other player sets up their

markers in quadrant fashion, but adjacent to the first player’s

quadrant with no Deep Space markers between them. Each

player places their Homeworlds in a hex marked with an “H”

in the diagram above.

• Starting Ships: Both players start with four SCs and four

Colony Ships instead of three of each. Other starting units

remain as per 1.1.1.

• Starting Tech: Both players start the game with Ship Size 2,

Ship Size 3, and Move 2 already researched.

2.10 2-PLAYER GALACTIC CAPITOL

• A 2-player game with five rows of Deep Space in between and

the Galactic Capitol in the center hex labeled “GC” (MRB 28.1)

• Including the Galactic Capitol there are a total of 43 Deep

Space markers.

• There are 26 Home System markers on each side. This means

that both players must choose one of the half-hexes on the

right edge of their playing area within their Home System. This

half-hex gets one of the System markers and is fully playable

during the game.

• This map can also be played without the Galactic Capitol.

2.11 2-PLAYER – SINGLE TALON MAP

• This scenario requires the use of the map board from the game

Talon, also available through GMT Games. Two Empires set

up on the opposite sides of the board as shown.

• There are 118 Deep Space markers.

• Partial hexes are not used.

text: 8 © 2024 GMT Games, LLC Space Empires: All Good Things - Competitive Scenario Book - Version 1.0 2.12 2-PLAYER – BOTH TALON MAPS • This scenario requires the use of both map boards from the game Talon, also available through GMT Games. The two boards are placed as above, with two Empires set up on the opposite sides of the map as shown. Each player’s Homeworld is placed in a hex marked with an “H”. Since there are 30 Home System hexes, each player will have to use 4 of the new Home System markers (marked with a “v” on the front side) at random. • There are 198 Deep Space markers. • Partial hexes are not used. 2.13 2-PLAYER – “THE BELT” (TALON MAP) 2.13.1 Setup • This scenario requires the use of the map board from the game Talon, also available separately through GMT Games. Two Empires set up on the opposite sides of the board as shown. Each player’s Homeworld is placed in a hex marked with an “H.” The Home System markers from a different Empire are placed in the red belt between the two Empires. Remove the Homeworld, one non-Barren Planet, one Asteroid counter, and one Nebula before mixing them. • There are 96 Deep Space markers 2.13.2 Special Rules • Partial hexes are not used. • Do not play with the Amazing Diplomats Empire Advantage. • Non-Player Aliens are on all Deep Space and Belt planets, including those that are not Barren. It is possible that you will run out of NPAs in this scenario. If that happens, use a spare counter to mark which planets still have NPAs and how many and draw specific ships when they are attacked. • All planets in Deep Space and the Belt give an Alien Technology Card when colonized (MRB 11.0). If you run out of Alien Technology Cards, reshuffle the discards to form a new draw pile. • It is recommended that Doomsday Machines (MRB 29.0) and Warp Points (MRB 28.2) are NOT used. 2.13.3 Victory Conditions: In addition to the normal victory conditions, a player can also win by having a Colony on six of the belt planets. Design Note: This is a longer game, not only because of the size of the map, but because any new Colonies in the belt will be vulnerable. It will be harder for one side to take a dominating lead and both sides should end up deeper in the tech tree. 2.14 2-PLAYER – “BELT AND VOID” (TALON MAP) 2.14.1 Setup: Everything is the same as The Belt scenario, except that the Deep Space hexes are referred to as the “Void.” Add Deep Space markers to every hex of the Void. 2.14.2 Special Rules • In the Void, (but not the Belt hexes), there are strange radiation and spatial properties that make it difficult for certain ships to enter. • Only Decoys, Minesweepers, Scouts, Destroyers, Raiders, Miners, Transports, Titans, and any advanced versions of those ships may

subject: Board Games / 4X Strategy / Space Empires 4X / Scenarios & Variants
text: hex of the Void. 2.14.2 Special Rules • In the Void, (but not the Belt hexes), there are strange radiation and spatial properties that make it difficult for certain ships to enter. • Only Decoys, Minesweepers, Scouts, Destroyers, Raiders, Miners, Transports, Titans, and any advanced versions of those ships may enter the Void. • Fighters and Ground Units that are carried by one of the above vessels (or on a Colony) are also allowed. • Space Pirates are allowed but captured Non-Player Alien ships are not allowed to move in the Void. • Bases and Shipyards can be built at Colonies in the Void and can move in the Void if using the On the Move Empire Advantage. • It is not recommended that this scenario be played with Replicators. Design Note: Taking a planet and establishing SYs in the belt to build bigger ships is the key to the game. Ground Units will need to be used since Colony Ships cannot enter the Void.

text: 9 © 2024 GMT Games, LLC Space Empires: All Good Things - Competitive Scenario Book - Version 1.0 3.0 3-Player Scenarios 3.1 3-PLAYER STANDARD • Each player sets up their 26 Home System markers in one of the regions normally used in a 3-Player game (denoted by the shading on the game board). • The rest of the map (including the two hexes “behind” Player 2 and Player 3) gets Deep Space markers. This will use 72 of them. • If all players agree, players may place their Homeworlds in any hex of their Home System. Otherwise, they use the hex marked with an “H”. 3.2 3-PLAYER VARIABLE • Each player takes their Homeworld and places it randomly. A 20-sided die can be used for this. If one is not available, then pieces of paper numbered 1 to 13 can be drawn out of a cup. The first number rolled/drawn is the row in which your Homeworld will be placed and the second number rolled/ drawn is the number of hexes counted over in that row. If a number is rolled that is out of range, then the die is re-rolled until one in range is achieved. • All other System markers, both Home System and Deep Space, are mixed together and randomly placed all over the map. • This will often lead to unbalanced map layouts. However, for those that love diplomacy, this is all part of the challenge. Those that have weaker positions often gang up on the strong. 3.3 3-PLAYER TEAMS – 2 VS 1 • Setup is the same as the 3-Player Standard scenario (3.1). • Mutually agree or determine randomly which two players will be on the same team versus the third player. • The two teammates operate under an UNEASY ALLIANCE (1.3.1). • A player is eliminated when their Homeworld is destroyed. The team wins as soon as they destroy the Homeworld of the single player. The single player must eliminate both of the other players in order to win. However, the single player does not get the 30 CP conquering bonus listed in the Multiplayer Long Game Victory Condition (1.1.8). • Each of the allied players collect income normally, but all of their ships and technology cost 1.5 times normal (after applying any modifiers from e.g. Alien Technology Cards (MRB 11.0), round up). For example, a Scout would cost 9 instead of 6, a Destroyer would cost 14 instead of 9, and Tactics 1 Technology would cost 23 instead of 15. • Maintenance and turn order bid would still be normal cost. 3.4 3-PLAYER TEAMS – 2 VS 1 WITH EMPIRE ADVANTAGES The setup and rules for this scenario are the same as given in the 3-Player Teams – 2 vs 1 scenario (3.3), with the following exceptions: • The team of two players pays the normal cost in CP for everything. They do not have to pay 1.5 times the normal amount. • The team of two players does not get any

text: given in the 3-Player Teams – 2 vs 1 scenario (3.3), with the following exceptions: • The team of two players pays the normal cost in CP for everything. They do not have to pay 1.5 times the normal amount. • The team of two players does not get any Empire Advantages (MRB 10.0), but the single player draws six Empire Advantages and gets to choose four of them. As normal, these Empire Advantages remain secret until used in such a way that they would be revealed or a Colony is invaded. 3.5 3-PLAYER TEAMS - 2 VS 1 WITH REPLICATORS • Setup is the same as the 3-Player Teams - 2 vs 1 scenario (3.3). • Mutually agree or determine trandomly which two players will be on the same team versus the third player. • The two teammates operate under an UNEASY ALLIANCE (1.3.1). • In this game the Replicator’s Colonies start at 1 CP instead of Start. They can still be bombarded back down to Start and will then grow to 1 CP – 3 CP – Full. • A player is eliminated when their Homeworld is destroyed. The team wins as soon as they destroy the Replicator player’s Homeworld. The Replicator player must eliminate both of the other players in order to win. However, the Replicator player does not get the 30 CP conquering bonus listed in the Multiplayer Long Game Victory Condition (1.1.8.)

4.0 4-Player Scenarios

4.1 4-PLAYER STANDARD

• Each player sets up their 26 Home System markers in one of

the regions normally used in a 4-Player game (denoted by the

outlined sections on the game board).

• The rest of the map gets Deep Space markers. This will use 46

of them.

4.2 4-PLAYER VARIABLE

• Setup is the same as the Variable 3-Player Map (3.2).

4.3 4-PLAYER TEAM PLAY

• Setup is the same as the Standard 4-Player Map (4.1).

• Mutually agree or determine randomly what the teams will be.

• Teammates can be diagonally across from each other or next

to one another depending on personal preference.

• Players must decide to play as an UNEASY ALLIANCE or as

BLOOD BROTHERS (1.3).

4.4 4-PLAYER TEAMS WITH EMPIRE

ADVANTAGES

• Setup is the same as 4-Player Team Play (4.3).

• All players draw Empire Advantages as per either normal

method (MRB 10.0).

4.5 4-PLAYER TEAMS – 3 VS 1

The setup and rules for this scenario are the same as given

in the 4-Player Team Play scenario (4.3), with the following

exceptions:

• Instead of 2 vs 2, this scenario is 3 vs 1.

• The three players that are on the same team are operating as

BLOOD BROTHERS (1.3.2).

• The player on their own receives double the normal CP income

from all Colonies except their Homeworld. Example: If a player

Homeworlds.

text: 15 © 2024 GMT Games, LLC Space Empires: All Good Things - Competitive Scenario Book - Version 1.0 7.3 4-PLAYER QUICK CONQUEST (REFER TO 4.1) 8.0 Epic Scenarios for 2-8 Players • These scenarios require two copies of the game and are played on two boards (see 4.10 for how to lay out two boards). • These are meant to enable a game with more than four players, but any of the scenario setups can be used for an epic scenario. The possibilities are numerous. - If six players are in the game, using the 3-Player Standard setup on both boards makes sense. - With seven players, using the 3-Player Standard setup on one board and the 4-Player Standard setup on the other would work. - A 2-Player game can be played where each player controls an empire on both maps. - A 4-Player game could be played with two players on each map using the “I Dare You to Play This” setup. In this case, it is recommended that each player use all the Colony Ships of their own color from both games. - An 8-Player game could be played with a Variable Map setup (3.2 or 4.2). • If more than four players are playing, then some of the same color playing pieces would have to be used. For example, there might be two green empires. Empires of the same color should be differentiated by starting the game on separate maps. When a unit crosses over maps, it should be marked with a spare token of some sort, e.g., a counter from some other game. • In an epic scenario, when one player conquers another, they add all the conquered player’s pieces to their build pool. • Warp Points: Warp Points (MRB 28.2) are the connection between the boards. All six Warp Point 1s (three on each board) are considered connected. The same with all six Warp Point 2s. Players should agree on the number of Warp Points used on each map (i.e. whether they want to discard the third warp point of each number as usual or keep them in play). Deep Space markers should be mixed so that the same number of Warp Points of the same ID (1 or 2) are on each map. 9.0 Planetary Gate Scenarios Planetary Gate scenarios are for those who can’t get enough ground combat. 9.1 SPECIAL RULES 9.1.1 Planetary Gates: Each planet is connected to any other planet within three hexes by means of a Planetary Gate. Ground Units (MRB 21.0) on one planet may move to any other planet within three hexes via this Planetary Gate. That is considered their entire move that turn. The Air Support Alien Technology card does not enable Transports to use Planetary Gates. Colonies built on asteroids by the Industrious Race are not connected by Planetary Gates. 9.1.2 Barren Planets: Barren Planets may be pre-placed in Deep Space between Home Systems so that it increases the likelihood that players are connected by Planetary

subject: Board Games / 4X Strategy / Space Empires 4X / Scenarios & Variants
text: Technology card does not enable Transports to use Planetary Gates. Colonies built on asteroids by the Industrious Race are not connected by Planetary Gates. 9.1.2 Barren Planets: Barren Planets may be pre-placed in Deep Space between Home Systems so that it increases the likelihood that players are connected by Planetary Gates. In the diagrams below, Barren planets are placed on the hexes marked with a “B.” These are suggestions for the 2-Player, 3-Player, and 4-Player game. However, players should feel free to play without pre-placement of planets or come up with some other mutually agreed upon layout. Players may also decide to remove all Barren Planets from the remaining Deep Space System markers to limit extra connectivity. 9.1.3 Combat through Gates: If Ground Units are moved by a Planetary Gate and the destination planet is colonized by another player or Non-Player Aliens (MRB 18.0), ground combat occurs normally. Regardless of the ground combat technology of the invading player, attacking units do not get to attack in the first round. The invasion and the result are handled the same as if the planet was invaded from space. 9.1.4 Retreats: Ground Units may retreat through gates, but only to friendly planets within three hexes that are free of enemy units. They may retreat before bombardment or during ground combat when it is their turn to fire, but not during the first round. Fighters may not retreat via Planetary Gates. 9.1.5 Alien Planets: An NPA planet may be captured by invading through a Planetary Gate. In that case, any alien ships still in orbit are removed from the game. 9.1.6 Gates and Fighters: Fighters (MRB 15.2) may move between planets like Ground Units, but only between friendly Colonies. 9.1.7 Colony Ships: When a Colony Ship is purchased, it may be purchased as a non-spacefaring Colony Ship. It still costs the same, but it can be placed, without the use of a Shipyard, on any Colony that produced 5 CP in that Economic Phase. It should be placed ship-side-up on top of the existing Colony. A non-spacefaring Colony Ship can never move into space but may travel though gates like Ground Units. It must colonize the first uncolonized planet that it moves to. The ship is left on the uncolonized planet and is flipped in the Economic Phase just as it would be if it was a normal Colony Ship.

10.0 Space Amoeba Scenarios

Giant Space Amoebas can be added to many of the competitive

scenarios in this book. During the game, the Space Amoebas

will multiply and attempt to destroy the human players. There

are similar rules for Space Amoebas included in the Solo/Co-op

Scenario Book (SSB 3.0).

10.1 SETUP

• Space Amoebas may be pre-placed in Deep Space between the

Home Systems. No Deep Space marker is placed in a hex with

an Amoeba.

• In the diagrams to follow, Space Amoebas are placed on the

hexes marked with an “S” as suggestions for the Large 2-Player

Map as well as the Standard 3-Player and 4-Player Maps.

However, players should feel free to play with some other

mutually agreed upon layout.

• All Space Amoebas are of one type (unless the players agree

upon some layout with multiple types). A Space Amoeba

counter of any of the three included types may be used.

10.2 SPECIAL RULES

• Unlike the solitary Space Amoeba scenario there are no rule

restrictions (i.e., Titans, Ground Combat, Facilities, etc. can

be used).

• Unlike the solitary Space Amoeba scenario players must pay

maintenance as usual.

• Unlike the solitary Space Amoeba scenario Barren Planets do

have Non-Player Aliens (MRB 18.0) on them.

• Like in the solitaire Space Amoeba scenario, Minesweepers

(SW) represent Science Vessels (SV), which are needed

to defeat the Amoebas. However, Minesweepers pull

double duty in the competitive scenario. Minesweeper

technology now also counts as Science and must be

researched to produce hybrid Minesweeper/Science Vessels. A

Minesweeper counter may both research Amoebas and sweep

subject: Board Games / 4X Strategy / Space Empires 4X / Scenarios & Variants
text: 17 © 2024 GMT Games, LLC Space Empires: All Good Things - Competitive Scenario Book - Version 1.0 Mines. Use the costs for Minesweeper ships and Minesweeper technology for producing these hybrid ships. • Only the first two levels of Minesweeper technology count as Science technology. Minesweeper 3 technology may be researched and used as normal, but it does not provide any additional bonus to researching Space Amoebas. • If a player is eliminated by an Amoeba, play continues for the remaining players until only one remains (the winner) or until a victory condition is met. Design Note: Yes, I know it says SW on the counters, but in this scenario, it is also as if they say SV, as in Science Vessels. Pretend that the SW was put on the counter by someone who heard the words “Science Vessel” spoken by someone with a bad TV Russian accent (I’m looking at you, Chekov). 10.3 RESEARCHING SPACE AMOEBAS • Until a player has researched the Amoebas, they cannot be attacked and will destroy all that player’s ships (except SVs) in the same hex. If SVs are present, they may research the Amoeba before it destroys the other ships. Once that player’s SVs gather 10 or more Research Points, their other ships will not be automatically destroyed. • Unlike the solitaire scenario, a player is not required to lose a ship to an Amoeba before researching them. • Each player must research the Space Amoebas individually. • Research Points carry over from turn to turn, and each player should track their own research completion. • As soon as an SV is in the same hex with an Amoeba, one or more dice are rolled for each SV to represent that ship’s research into the Amoebas. - 1-2: Two Research Points gained. - 3-5: One Research Point gained. - 6-8: Nothing. - 9-10: SV destroyed. • Level 1 Science Vessels roll one die when researching. • Level 2 Science Vessels get to roll two dice and use the best result. • Level 2 Science/Minesweeper technology can’t be acquired until a Level 1 Science Vessel has researched an Amoeba (successful or not). • Once a player has a total of 10 Research Points, that player may attack any Amoeba they encounter for the rest of the game. • A Science Vessel may research an Amoeba on the same turn the ship moves into its hex. • Multiple SVs can research each turn they are in a hex with an Amoeba. • If an SV shares a hex with any Amoeba after they are completely researched, that ship still must roll a die for research as normal and may be destroyed on a roll of 9-10. No further Research Points are gained. • After 10 Research Points have been gathered by a player, the Amoebas will no longer automatically destroy their ships. Instead, combat ensues as normal. However, Science Vessels never participate in combat with an Amoeba. 10.4 SPACE AMOEBA STRENGTH After a player

subject: Board Games / 4X Strategy / Space Empires 4X / Scenarios & Variants
text: of 9-10. No further Research Points are gained. • After 10 Research Points have been gathered by a player, the Amoebas will no longer automatically destroy their ships. Instead, combat ensues as normal. However, Science Vessels never participate in combat with an Amoeba. 10.4 SPACE AMOEBA STRENGTH After a player has gathered 10 Research Points, it means they have figured out how to kill the Amoebas. Design Note: In sci-fi terms, the Amoeba can be killed if the player slightly tweaks their weapons – by adjusting the frequency to the upper EM band, by adjusting the phase, by reversing the polarity, or by doing something else pseudo-scientific sounding. The specific adjustment is not important. It means it is now possible to (immediately) attack them. No retrofit (technology upgrade) is needed for ships in order to attack (MRB 9.11.3). 10.4.1 Determine Amoeba Strength • The first player to gather 10 Research Points should roll on the Amoeba Strength Table. This result will apply to all players, but the player who rolled should not reveal the results to other players until they have completed their own research (they may conduct combat vs Amoebas in secrecy). • The results rolled on the Amoeba Strength Table determine the type for all Amoebas in the game. 10.5 AMOEBA STRENGTH TABLE Roll Amoeba Type 1 3 5 8-10 6-7 4 2 C7-1-1 D6-2-2 A7-2-2. The Amoebas are also vulnerable to fighters. B5-1-2. The Amoebas have voracious appetites and gets two attacks each round. Each of these attacks may lead to a Chain Attack (10.6.3). The Amoebas can only be killed by detonating a ship inside them. Only one ship may attempt this per turn. If the ship is a CA, BC, BB, DN, or Titan, the Amoeba is destroyed. If the ship is a DD or MB the Amoeba is destroyed on a roll of 1-8 and if the ship is a SC the Amoeba is destroyed on a roll of 1-4. In all cases, the ship attempting this is destroyed. Only the ships listed here can be used. If the Amoeba survives, other ships in the hex with the Amoeba are destroyed. A7-2-2. In any round in which there are 3 or more different types of ships (for example: SC, DD, and CA) attacking the Amoebas, all attacking ships get +1 to their attack. A7-2-2. The Amoebas become an A5-1-2 when faced by ONLY Raiders.

subject: Board Games / 4X Strategy / Space Empires 4X / Scenarios & Variants
text: 18 © 2024 GMT Games, LLC Space Empires: All Good Things - Competitive Scenario Book - Version 1.0 10.5.1 Explanation of Strength Table Results • On a result of 8-10, no adjustment to weapons will work on the Amoebas and the only way to destroy one is by detonating a ship inside of it. • Any other result means it is now possible to attack them and this is the Amoebas’ Attack-Defense-Hull Size in combat. 10.6 AMOEBA PROPERTIES • All Amoebas have a Tactics Level of 2. • All Amoebas are immune to Cloaking and Fighters unless otherwise specified. • Ships never get a Fleet Size Bonus (MRB 5.1.3) against Amoebas. 10.6.1 Amoeba Target Priority: Each time an Amoeba attacks, these mindless denizens of the interstellar void roll a die to determine which ship they will attempt to swallow (attack). On a roll of 1-5 it will attack the smallest ship in combat. On a roll of 6-10 it will attack the largest ship in the combat. Use the CP value of a ship to determine largest and smallest. Since Flagships are irreplaceable, they are considered to have the highest CP value. If there is a tie, the player chooses the target of the Space Amoeba. If a player chooses to, they may allow their SVs to be attacked (sometimes useful after the Amoebas have been researched as the SVs have a chance to be destroyed anyway. 10.6.2 Amoeba Attack Procedure: The Amoeba rolls one die and executes the attack normally, comparing its Attack Strength to the ship’s Defense Strength (including technology). However, larger ships are even harder to swallow. Ships with a Hull Size of 2 get an extra +1 to their Defense Strength and ships with a Hull Size of 3 (or larger) get an extra +2 to their Defense Strength. A successful attack results in the ship being swallowed (destroyed) regardless of size. As always, a roll of 1 is a successful attack and will swallow a ship, regardless of Defense Strength and size. 10.6.3 Chain Attacks: If an Amoeba successfully swallows a ship, and rolls 2 or less than was needed, then the extra energy gained from swallowing the ship allows the Amoeba to immediately attack again. This can happen any number of times during a round of combat. EXAMPLE: A Type 4 Amoeba (A7-2-2) is being attacked by four Scouts, a Cruiser, and a Battleship. All of the ships have Defense Technology 1. The Amoeba attacks first. It rolls a 3 and so will attack the smallest ship (a Scout). On its attack, it rolls a 4. Since it needed a 6, the Amoeba not only swallows the Scout but gets to attack again. This time it rolls a 6 and will attack the largest ship (the Battleship). The Battleship has a Defense Strength of 5 (Defense of 2 + Defense Tech 1 + 2 because of Hull Size 3). The Amoeba rolls a 1, swallowing the Battleship. However, since it was only one less

subject: Board Games / 4X Strategy / Space Empires 4X / Scenarios & Variants
text: time it rolls a 6 and will attack the largest ship (the Battleship). The Battleship has a Defense Strength of 5 (Defense of 2 + Defense Tech 1 + 2 because of Hull Size 3). The Amoeba rolls a 1, swallowing the Battleship. However, since it was only one less than was needed, it does not get to make a further chain attack. 10.6.4 Amoeba Healing: After combat, damage to an Amoeba is healed in the same way as ships. 10.7 SPREADING OF SPACE AMOEBAS Amoebas never move. Once placed, an Amoeba counter never moves to a new hex. Instead, Amoebas spread at the start of the Economic Phase before players make any purchases. 10.7.1 Spread Procedure: Amoeba spreading is not random. The following guidelines and restrictions apply to the spreading: • Space Amoebas do not start spreading until Economic Phase 7 (or a different phase upon the mutual agreement of all players). • Only one Amoeba may ever be in a hex. • Each Amoeba may only spread one time into one hex during an Economic Phase. • In turn order, each player picks an Amoeba and chooses the hex that it will spread to, placing a new Amoeba in that hex. This process continues until all Amoebas have spread once. • If an Amoeba can spread onto a planet, when selected for spreading, it must spread to the planet hex. If it can spread to multiple planets, the person selecting it can choose which planet hex it spreads to. The presence or absence of a Colony is not a factor. • Newly placed Amoebas do not spread. New Amoebas placed that Economic Phase should be marked in some way so that they are not accidentally selected to spread. • Because Amoebas are attracted to the energy signature of the players’ Empires, they will never spread outside of the map area denoted by the chosen scenario map. • Space Amoebas will not spread into the same hex as a Doomsday Machine and a DM will not move into or through a hex with an Amoeba. • Amoebas are not limited by the number of counters available. Use substitutions when necessary. 10.7.2 Amoeba Contact with Ships: If there are ships in the hex in which an Amoeba spreads, a special combat round occurs immediately during the Economic Phase, with the Amoeba as the attacker. If a player with ships in that hex has not gathered 10 Research Points, all their ships that are not Science Vessels are destroyed (SVs get a Research roll). Space Amoebas automatically destroy neutral Non-Player Alien ships when they spread into their hex. 10.7.3 Amoeba Contact with System Markers: An Amoeba removes the System marker or Terrain Tile (MRB 6.10, whether explored or not) in any hex in which it spreads (only after defeating any ships present). If a hex has not been explored, the Amoeba spreads normally and the System marker is removed, sight unseen. An Amoeba will not spread into Black Holes (MRB 6.3)

subject: Board Games / 4X Strategy / Space Empires 4X / Scenarios & Variants
text: Terrain Tile (MRB 6.10, whether explored or not) in any hex in which it spreads (only after defeating any ships present). If a hex has not been explored, the Amoeba spreads normally and the System marker is removed, sight unseen. An Amoeba will not spread into Black Holes (MRB 6.3) or Supernovas (MRB 6.5). Amoebas skip hexes with Folds In Space (MRB 25.2) as normal, but in all other cases, Amoebae are totally unaffected by System markers.

10.8 SPACE AMOEBAS AND MINES

Space Amoebas are hindered from spreading by Mines.

However, Mines never affect Amoebas like they do ships.

10.8.1 Procedure: If a player moves only a Mine (or a Mine

and Science Vessels) into the same hex as an Amoeba, no combat

occurs. In the Economic Phase, the Mine is removed, and that

Amoeba does not spread during that one Economic Phase. No

other harm comes to the Amoeba.

10.8.2 Contact with Mines: Amoebas may spread onto Mines

normally; in which case the Mines have no effect but will remain

in the hex until the next Economic Phase when they may stop the

Amoeba from spreading as noted above. A Mine only stops an

Amoeba from spreading if it is in the same hex as the Amoeba

at the start of the Economic Phase.

10.8.3 Adapting to Mines: Amoebas will eventually

adapt to the use of Mines. Once each Economic Phase

an Amoeba does not spread because of any player’s

Mine, roll a die. On a result of 9-10, Amoebas will no

longer be prevented from spreading by Mines in future Economic

Phases. At the conclusion of every Economic Phase, a Mine will be

immediately destroyed when occupying a hex with an Amoeba.

Place a “No Mine” counter on one of the Amoebas as a reminder

that Mines no longer affect them.

text: 20 © 2024 GMT Games, LLC Space Empires: All Good Things - Competitive Scenario Book - Version 1.0 11.0 Alternate Victory Scenarios Both of the following alternate victory scenarios can be played in conjunction with almost every scenario in the game. A player still wins by being the first to destroy a Homeworld, but there is also another way to win through Victory Points (VPs). You can’t focus so much on the other way to win that you let your defense go slack. Use victory point chips or money from another game or scrap paper to track VPs. Everyone’s total score is always known and is open information. 11.1 KING OF THE WARP POINT The Warp Points are the key to victory in this scenario. They represent access to vast scientific treasures and ancient civilizations. By studying them, players unlock knowledge that will lead them to make galaxy changing advances. 11.1.1 Setup: Before the Deep Space markers are placed on the map, the Warp Points (MRB 28.2) are taken out of the mix. Depending on the scenario, a number of Warp Points are placed in mutually agreed upon hexes in the Deep Space portion of the map. Some guidelines on Warp Point placement: • It is strongly suggested that you use either: - One Warp Point, or - One less Warp Point than the number of players, or - One more Warp Point than the number of players. • All of the Warp Points can be placed centrally, or one could be placed to the advantage of each player with an extra one in the middle. There are a lot of options, and each will change the game. Design Note: Each of the above will make for a very different and interesting game – is it important to hold one Warp Point or do I need to hold several? If the players cannot agree on the placement of the Warp Points, one player should place all the Warp Points while the other players choose their starting positions once the Warp Points have been placed. Unused Warp Points should be placed to the side and not mixed in with the rest of the Deep Space Markers. 11.1.2 Warp Point Properties: None of the Warp Points in this scenario are connected. Their only purpose is to provide VPs. 11.1.3 Minesweepers: These ships have a dual function in this scenario. They not only sweep Mines, but also act as Science Vessels. You must still research Minesweeping Technology to build SWs (MRB 17.2). 11.1.4 Game Length: Players should decide at the start of the game how many Economic Phases they want the game to last. The game ends after that Economic Phase. 10-12 Econ Phases are good for a shorter game, depending on the scenario. This scenario also works with a longer game of 15-20 Econ Phases, although players in that one will have to be careful not to become too focused on the Warp Points as it will become more and more likely

subject: Board Games / 4X Strategy / Space Empires 4X / Scenarios & Variants
text: Phases are good for a shorter game, depending on the scenario. This scenario also works with a longer game of 15-20 Econ Phases, although players in that one will have to be careful not to become too focused on the Warp Points as it will become more and more likely that someone will launch a game-winning attack against a Homeworld. 11.1.5 Victory Points: Players score 1 VP during the Economic Phase for each Warp Point that shares a hex with one or more of their Science Vessels. All Science Vessels in the same hex as a Warp Point must be revealed during the Economic Phase. The player with the most VPs at the end of the agreed upon number of Economic Phases is the winner. In the event of a tie, after the purchases for the Economic Phase are completed, the players add up the CP value of all combat-capable ships they have on the board and the winner is the player whose fleet adds up to the most CP. In the event of another tie, the tied players share the win. 11.1.6 Alternate Point Scoring: As a twist to this scenario, players can agree that they will score 1 VP per Science Vessel at a Warp Point in the Economic Phase. This will change the scenario strategy significantly (weighing combat capability versus number of Science Vessels is tough) and it is recommended that this version is played with only one Warp Point. 11.2 GRAB THE ARTIFACTS Ancient artifacts of great value litter Deep Space and players must gather and study them. 11.2.1 Setup: Extra pieces or counters will be needed for this scenario (pieces from an old Risk game will work well). One of these extra pieces is placed in every Deep Space hex (along with the exploration marker). These will represent the ancient artifacts. 11.2.2 Exploring Artifacts: After exploration, artifacts remain in the hex even if the System marker is removed (Danger!, etc.). Exception: Artifacts in Supernovas (MRB 6.5), Folds In Space (MRB 25.2), and Quantum Filaments (MRB 25.5) are removed. 11.2.3 Moving Artifacts: Artifacts may only be moved by Miners. Place the artifact on the Miner counter when a Miner picks it up. They may pick up an artifact at any point during their move. The following rules apply to moving artifacts: • Each Miner may carry only one artifact at a time and may not carry minerals or Space Wrecks at the same time. • Artifacts being towed by Miners are destroyed if the Miner is destroyed. • Miners may drop off artifacts at their Homeworld or at a Colony that is connected by MS Pipelines to the Homeworld (it is assumed a civilian ship is able to transport the artifact from there). Miners may not drop off artifacts at Colonies that are not yet connected in this way. 11.2.4 Victory Conditions: Each artifact dropped off is worth 1 VP the instant it is dropped off. The game ends immediately (even in the middle of a

subject: Board Games / 4X Strategy / Space Empires 4X / Scenarios & Variants
text: able to transport the artifact from there). Miners may not drop off artifacts at Colonies that are not yet connected in this way. 11.2.4 Victory Conditions: Each artifact dropped off is worth 1 VP the instant it is dropped off. The game ends immediately (even in the middle of a turn) once someone has scored enough VPs that there is no possible way that their total may be passed by another player given the artifacts remaining on the board. In the event of a tie, the person who achieved their Victory Point total first is declared the winner.

text: 21 © 2024 GMT Games, LLC Space Empires: All Good Things - Competitive Scenario Book - Version 1.0 12.0 Taking A Stand (2-Player Variant) The following variants add the use of plastic stands which either provide more access to information or add an additional element of hidden information to the game. 12.1 STANDING SHIPS VARIANT • Unrevealed ships are not placed face down as in the normal game. Instead, ships are placed upright in stands facing the player. • The number of ships in each group is tracked on the Ship Technology Sheet. • When a group is revealed, it is placed face up and a numeral marker placed underneath. • Groups still have a limit of six ships. 12.2 2-PLAYER HIDDEN EXPLORATION VARIANT 12.2.1 Changes to Exploration • The map is set up normally, with face down System markers. • Deep Space markers must be explored by both sides. When a Deep Space System marker is explored or would be returned face up with Exploration technology (MRB 9.8) for the first time, it is not flipped face up and it may not be left face down. Instead, it is placed upright in a stand facing the exploring player. Some markers will be facing one player and some the other, depending on who explored it. If a marker is explored by the other player, it is then removed from the stand and placed face up. All markers that have been explored by both players will be face up on the board. • When a Deep Space System marker is explored by a ship for the first time, a Decoy (MRB 8.3) may be added to the hex. Players may scuttle another decoy during the exploration phase to accommodate this. After looking at the System marker and placing it in a stand, the player removes either the Decoy or the exploring ship. There will be one marker left in the hex, but the other player will not know whether it is a Decoy or a ship and therefore will be less likely to know if the Exploration went well. - If the System marker indicates that the hex is safe, the player removes the Decoy. - If the System marker indicates the ship’s destruction, the player removes the ship. 12.2.2 Special Rules • Deep Space Black Holes (MRB 6.3) are 100% deadly until explored by both players. If not face up or in a stand, a Deep Space Black Hole will always suck in the first exploring ship(s) (no need to roll the die). When this occurs, the Decoy marker is left behind. Future ships entering the hex roll the die as normal (which will give away that this is a Black Hole) • Alien planets (MRB 18.0) are also 100% deadly if attacked by a lone ship. The first time an alien planet is discovered and placed in a stand, it is treated like a Danger! marker and the exploring ship is removed without die rolls (leaving the Decoy marker

subject: Board Games / 4X Strategy / Space Empires 4X / Scenarios & Variants
text: Black Hole) • Alien planets (MRB 18.0) are also 100% deadly if attacked by a lone ship. The first time an alien planet is discovered and placed in a stand, it is treated like a Danger! marker and the exploring ship is removed without die rolls (leaving the Decoy marker behind). NPA ships are not chosen and placed in the hex until both sides have explored the planet or it is attacked by more than one ship. • Lost in Space markers (MRB 6.6) are treated like Danger! markers when first explored. Future ships from the same player entering the hex later ignore the effect. • Space Pirates (MRB 25.7) are turned face up and handled normally. • Regional Maps (MRB 25.6) are turned face up and handled normally (which could cause adjacent markers to be put in stands). • Minerals (MRB 6.7) and Space Wrecks (MRB 6.8) are placed in stands. However, if that player sends a Miner into the hex, it can be removed from the stand and picked up normally. • Supernovas (MRB 6.5) are treated like Danger! markers (MRB 6.4) when first explored. If explored by a ship, the Decoy is left behind in the hex and must be moved out during the next turn. It is possible that this could cause a Decoy to pass through a Supernova and out the other side under this one exception. The player that has explored the Supernova may not send ships into the hex. • Folds In Space (MRB 25.2) have no effect when first explored, remove the Decoy. Future ships may either enter the hex and ignore the usual effects or move through it. Once a ship moves through it remove the marker from the stand and place it face up. • Quantum Filaments (MRB 25.5) are treated like Danger! markers when first explored. Future ships from the same player may enter, but must spend an extra movement hex as normal. • Note that a player may ignore beneficial terrain effects (e.g. Pulsars MRB 25.4) to keep the terrain tile hidden from their opponent. 12.2.3 Game Effects • This variant creates paths that can be easily traversed by one player (because they’ve explored it), but not the other. That will at times allow a fleet to more easily retreat or avoid combat. • Both players are more likely to research Exploration technology because they will not be able to easily use a path made by the other player. • The cost of suffering a bad exploration result is slightly less because the player will receive a Decoy as a result. • There will be more Decoys on the board.

text: 23 © 2024 GMT Games, LLC Space Empires: All Good Things - Competitive Scenario Book - Version 1.0 Expanded Sequence of Play This is NOT meant to be used in normal game play. This is meant to resolve any disputes or confusion integrating all rules from the base game and all three expansions. It is meant to be a final arbiter on any possible confusion. Honestly, you will probably never need this, but it is here if you do. 1. Determine Player Order (first turn of the game only) 2. Turn One: Player 1: a. Movement. b. Combat. c. Exploration. Note that if Exploration results in hostile forces in the same hex return to Step b. immediately and resolve the combat now. Player(s) 2-8: Same as Player 1. After all players have had a turn any Doomsday Machines on the map move. 3. Turn Two: Same as turn one. 4. Turn Three: Same as turn one. 5. Economic Phase – All players conduct this phase simultaneously, not in player order. Exception to that is Resource Cards, which are played at the Start of the Economic Phase in Reverse Player Order. MOVEMENT Units may move from one hex to another at up to the maximum speed they are equipped with. This movement may be increased by MS Pipelines or Black Hole Slingshots, but not both. This movement may be reduced by entering various terrain types, hexes that contain enemy units, and hexes that are beside enemy units equipped with Advanced Comm Array. Ships use Exploration technology before moving. One or more Resource Cards may be played after Exploration tech is used but before any ship movement. Raiders vs. Scanners: When the attacker moves into a hex with enemy units and the attacker has Raiders present that they wish to continue moving, the following happens. (Note that though these rules refer to Raiders, they also cover any other ships equipped with Cloaking.) • The attacker states that they have Raiders and want to continue moving. • The defender states that they have Scanner 1 (and the attacker must stop) or that they lack Scanner 1 (and the attacker may continue moving). • If the defender stated that they had Scanner 1 the attacker may declare Cloaking 2. • The defender states that they have Scanner 2 (and that the attacker must stop) or that they lack Scanner 2 (and the attacker may continue moving). The defender has the option of not announcing their Scanner level and allowing the Raiders to continue to move. Tech levels must be announced in combat (See Step C8), but combat has not been declared at this point. Black Hole Survival Checks: These happen before Reaction Movement is declared and before non-combat ships are destroyed. A ship with Advanced Comm Array cannot React into a black hole hex if the enemy ship is attempting a Black Hole Slingshot. That ship can still React to the next hex the enemy ship enters, if that hex is adjacent to the

subject: Board Games / 4X Strategy / Space Empires 4X / Scenarios & Variants
text: declared and before non-combat ships are destroyed. A ship with Advanced Comm Array cannot React into a black hole hex if the enemy ship is attempting a Black Hole Slingshot. That ship can still React to the next hex the enemy ship enters, if that hex is adjacent to the ship making the Reaction Movement. If the attacker uses the Black Hole Slingshot rule to move to another hex any non-combat ships are destroyed before the attacking ships leave. NON-COMBAT SHIPS SPECIAL MOVEMENT RULES Units without an Attack Strength may not enter hexes that contain enemy units, enemy Colonies, or face-down System markers unless a friendly combat- capable ship also enters the hex. Units without an Attack Strength are destroyed if caught alone in a hex by combat-capable enemy units. No information about the enemy units is revealed by this except for the fact that they are armed. There are two exceptions to this rule: • Units without an Attack Strength are not destroyed if they are in a Black Hole hex and all of the enemy units fail their Black Hole survival checks. • Units without an Attack Strength and not in a Black Hole hex are not destroyed if friendly combat-capable units declare that they will use Advanced Comm Array to React into the hex. COMBAT C1 Combat-capable units or Colonies from two opposing sides are in the same hex after a player has finished moving. C2 Defender may move ships by Reaction Movement if they have the technology needed. C3 • Attacker declares whether they have Cloaking 1 or not. • Defender declares whether they have Scanner 1 or not. • If defender has Scanner 1, attacker declares if they have Cloaking 2 or not. • If attacker declared they have Cloaking 2, then defender declares if they have Scanner 2 or not. C4 • Defender declares whether they have Cloaking 1 or not. • Attacker declares that they have Scanner 1 or not. • If attacker has Scanner 1, defender declares if they have Cloaking 2 or not. • If defender declared they have Cloaking 2 then attacker declares if they have Scanner 2 or not. C5 Play Resource cards that are played before ships are revealed (Missed Rendezvous, Retreat When Engaged, and Alien Reinforcements). Raiders and Missed Rendezvous: If Missed Rendezvous causes a situation in which there are no ships equipped with Scanner until Round 2, there are three possibilities: 1. If the Raider side has nothing but Raiders and they are trying to avoid combat by staying cloaked after learning this, then the battle does

subject: Board Games / 4X Strategy / Space Empires 4X / Scenarios & Variants
text: 24 © 2024 GMT Games, LLC Space Empires: All Good Things - Competitive Scenario Book - Version 1.0 not start until the Scanner-equipped ship arrives and Round 2 will count as Round 1 for most purposes. The Raiders will still be able to retreat at the first D-Class firing opportunity they get. 2. If the Raider side has Raiders that are trying to avoid combat by staying cloaked AND ships that are not Raiders, then Round 1 is fought as normal, but the Raiders cannot fire or be fired at. In Round 2, the Raiders can be fired at and can retreat or fire at the first D-Class firing opportunity they get. 3. If the Raiders declare that they want the +1 in Round 1, then Round 1 is fought as normal. The Raiders will fire as A-Class Units with a +1 to their Attack Strength. In Round 2, the Raiders will be treated as D-Class Units. C6 Reveal ship types and numbers. Attacker first and then the defender. At this point, if a Colony is present the defender must state which of their Ground Units are on the Colony and which are on Transports. C7 Attacking Raiders that have not had their cloak nullified may retreat. Defending Raiders that have not had their cloak nullified may retreat. C8 Both sides reveal what tech is on their units and some Empire Advantages and Alien Technology Cards are revealed. Both sides reveal the Experience levels that their units have. All special abilities that Unique Ships and Advanced Flagships mount must be revealed. Ground 2, Terraforming 1-2, and Military Academy 1 are not revealed in space combat. All other technology is revealed. The following Alien Tech Cards must be revealed if a unit mounts them: 2, 3, 5, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 22, 24, 25, 26, 27*, 28, 29, 30, 56, 183, 185, 186, 282, 283. Note for 27: Only revealed if a mobile unit is in combat. The following Empire Advantages have to be revealed if non-Ground Units from these Empires are in the battle: 31*, 32*, 33*, 34, 38, 40*, 42*, 43, 44, 45, 46, 47*, 50*, 51*, 52*, 53, 54*, 55*, 58*, 59*, 62*, 63*, 187*, 188. Note for 31 & 32: Boarding Ships do not force a reveal. Note for 33: Bases and Shipyards do not force a reveal. Note for 40: Only units that can gain Experience force a reveal. Note for 42: A mobile unit must be present to force a reveal. Note for 47: Only reveal if Boarding Ships are present from either side or Ground Units including Militia are present from the And We Still Carry Swords side. Note for 50: A SC/DD/CA that mounts Cloaking must be present to force the reveal. Note for 51: Units that get the Empire Advantage must be present to force the reveal. Note for 52: A Mine must be present to force a reveal. Note for 54: A unit that mounts Exploration 1 must

subject: Board Games / 4X Strategy / Space Empires 4X / Scenarios & Variants
text: A SC/DD/CA that mounts Cloaking must be present to force the reveal. Note for 51: Units that get the Empire Advantage must be present to force the reveal. Note for 52: A Mine must be present to force a reveal. Note for 54: A unit that mounts Exploration 1 must be present to force the reveal. Note for 55: Reveal is optional to avoid revealing technology. Note for 58: A Shipyard or Base must be present to force the reveal. Note for 59: Fighters and A-Class units do not force a reveal. Note for 62: Only ships that benefit from this card force a reveal. Note for 63: Only ships that benefit from this card force a reveal. Note for 187: A Titan must be present to force a reveal. C9 The attacker declares if any of their Raiders that were avoiding combat by staying cloaked are going to join in the combat now instead. Any Raiders that do join the battle at this time also have to reveal their tech. The attacker declares if any of their units with Anti-Sensor Hull are turning off that technology for this battle. The attacker designates which units they are protecting with Shield Projectors. C10 The defender declares if any of their Raiders that were avoiding combat by staying cloaked are going to join in the combat now instead. Any Raiders that do join the battle at this time also have to reveal their tech. The defender designates which units they are protecting with Shield Projectors. C11 Play Cards after ships are revealed (Cancel Card–Red Squadron, Cancel Card–Sensor Blind Spot, Cancel Card–Concealed Minefield, 4 Heroic Ship Cards, 2 Forced System Shutdowns & Overconfidence). C12 Sweep Mines. C13 Mines detonate. C14 The player with more units can determine which ones they wish to screen this round. Note that the Shape Shifter Empire Advantage may be revealed at this time to activate the once per game effect. C15 Ships equipped with Tractor Beam declare which ships they are preventing from retreating this round. Attacking player declares first. C16 Round 1 of combat is conducted as laid out in the rules. A-Class rated defenders with Tactics 3 will fire first. All Raiders that have an un-nullified cloak will get a +1 to their Attack Strength this round. Units must choose to fire or not fire this round. C17 Horsemen of the Plains may retreat. C18 Celestial Knights may declare a charge. C19 Screened ships are re-determined. Tractor beam targets are re-determined. Round 2 of combat is conducted as laid out in the rules.

subject: Board Games / 4X Strategy / Space Empires 4X / Scenarios & Variants
text: 25 © 2024 GMT Games, LLC Space Empires: All Good Things - Competitive Scenario Book - Version 1.0 A-Class rated defenders with Tactics 3 will fire first. Units may fire, not fire, or retreat this round. C20 The attacker may drop Ground Units onto an enemy Colony if no Mines remain. The defender may drop Ground Units onto a friendly Colony. C21 Horsemen of the Plains may retreat. C22 Celestial Knights may declare a charge if they have not already done so. C23 Continue with steps C19 through C22 until one side has no more space combat-capable units. Then destroy any non-combat units that do not have friendly space combat-capable units remaining. Play Coup card. C24 Play Planetary Bombardment Cards. C25 Conduct Planetary Bombardment if no Mines remain. All units that are not cloaked to avoid combat must reveal their Tech/Empire Advantages as per C8 if they have not already done so. C26 Play Ground Combat Cards (Heroic Ground Unit and Defending Familiar Terrain). C27 Conduct Ground Combat if no Mines remain. C28 If the Colony is captured, reduce its CP value by one step but not below Colony Ship level. Remove one of the invader’s Ground Units to form a garrison. Remove from the hex any Minerals, Space Wrecks, or Facilities that were on the planet during the battle. Remove from the hex any Fighters that were in the battle that the surviving ships cannot support. The player who lost the Colony must reveal all their Empire Advantage and Alien Tech Cards to the player who captured the Colony. The player who lost the Colony must reveal their Production Sheet to the capturing player. Based on where the planet is on the map, the capturing player may steal one or two technologies that the other player has researched. EXPLORATION Units that share a space with a face down System marker must explore that System. Flip the marker over and reveal its identity to all players. Apply effects immediately. ECONOMIC PHASE E1 Depending on Turn and Empire Advantage, the Replicators may have to remove a Colony marker and the planet it is on from the map. E2 Each player may scrap Facilities. This happens before any CP/RP/LP/TP are generated. E3 On even-numbered Economic Phases, each player draws two Crew Cards from the appropriate deck, chooses one to keep face down, and discards the other. E4 All players may draw one Resource Card if their Resource Card deck is not exhausted. E5 In reverse Player order, the players may play Resource Cards. E6 In reverse Player order, the players may discard and activate Cancel Cards. E7 Players collect CP/RP/LP/TP and receive free Ground Units if eligible. E8 All players determine their Supply status, then may add/remove Supply Groups to the board. E9 Players record penalty CPs if necessary. E10 Space Amoebas spread. This may result in immediate combat. E11 Players spend income. E12 Players place new units on the map. E13 Players must assign or discard Crew Cards selected during this

text: Supply status, then may add/remove Supply Groups to the board. E9 Players record penalty CPs if necessary. E10 Space Amoebas spread. This may result in immediate combat. E11 Players spend income. E12 Players place new units on the map. E13 Players must assign or discard Crew Cards selected during this Economic Phase. E14 Players adjust Colony counters to reflect growth. E15 Players announce bids for turn order. E16 Players discard Resource Cards that they wish to use for their CP value. Players may also play the Research Breakthrough Card. E17 Players discard Resource Cards that are over the hand limit of six cards.

subject: Board Games / 4X Strategy / Space Empires 4X / Scenarios & Variants
text: 26 © 2024 GMT Games, LLC Space Empires: All Good Things - Competitive Scenario Book - Version 1.0 Comprehensive Example of Combat In this example, a Yellow fleet of 15 combat-capable ships and one non-combat ship enters a hex with a Green fleet and Colony. All units have been revealed and Decoys have been removed. The number of each ship is indicated by the number below the group markers. Detonate Mines: Yellow has not brought along any Minesweepers so the Mine in the Green fleet detonates. The Green player selects a CV as the Mine’s target. Since there is now only 1 CV left, 3 of the Fighters are also destroyed. This is a huge loss for the Yellow fleet. The Green Mine is removed from play. Combat Screening: Next, players form their battle lines. Yellow has 11 combat-capable ships remaining versus 9 Green combat-capable ships – Yellow may therefore screen two of their own ships. The Colony Ship is a non-combat ship and is automatically screened until the end of the battle and doesn’t count against this limit. Their CV cannot be targeted until all their Fighters are destroyed, so Yellow doesn’t screen any ships. Note that Green’s Colony cannot be attacked until all of Green’s ships have been destroyed or retreated. Fleet Size Bonus: Neither side has twice as many ships as the other, so neither player receives this +1 bonus. Technologies: Both players have the same Tactics Level, so ties in Weapon Class ratings will be decided in the defender’s favor. Both players have Fighter Technology 1, which means their Fighters are B-Class, Attack 5, and Defense 0. Neither player has invested in Attack or Defense Technologies, so Attack and Defense Strengths will be unmodified. Both players have Level 1 Cloaking. Neither has Scanner Technology to negate Cloaking but the Yellow Raiders still opt to participate in the combat. Neither player has Point Defense. Green Fires A-Class: First to fire will be the defender’s A-Class rated ships, but they have only one – their Base. Green fires their Base (Attack 7) against one of the Raiders (Defense 0). The Base will hit the Raider on a roll of 1-7. A 5 is rolled, so the Raider group receives a Hit marker. Since it takes two hits to eliminate each Raider, the Hit marker has no effect until a second hit is applied against the Raider group. Yellow Fires A-Class: Now it’s Yellow’s turn to fire their A-Class rated ships. Since Green does not have Scanner Technology, the 2 Yellow Raiders are considered Cloaked and therefore have an A-Class rating. Cloaking 1 gives them an Attack Strength of 4. During this first round of combat (only), they get a +1 to their Attack Strength. Both fire on the Battlecruiser group, needing a 1-4 to hit (5-1=4). The die rolls are 10 and 8 – both misses. Since Yellow still has the majority of combat-capable ships (8 to 6), they elect to screen their CV since it is now vulnerable without the Fighters. In the second round of combat, ships may now retreat instead of firing – retreating takes place at the moment the ship can fire. First up to fire is the Green Base and fire proceeds in the same order as the first round. The battle proceeds for as many rounds as it takes until one player is destroyed or all their units retreat. In this example, Yellow may have a slight advantage, but the battle is bound to be bloody for each side.
